title: Music Annual Update _ CQIP 2018-2019 SY (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
Recruitment (The following items create a relationship between the school music
department and the community including potential future COS students)
● Master of Ceremonies for the Red Scarf Society for the Performing Arts concert (Yreka):
M5 Brass
● Two new ensembles added beginning Spring of 2019
○ Yreka Concert Choir - to provide access for North County musicians who are
unable to drive to the main campus
○ Beginning Vocal Jazz Ensemble - to provide access for the growing number of
students interested in participating in Vocal Jazz
● Vocal Jazz Ensemble Tour continues to develop relationships with future COS students
○ 7 schools in Redding, Lower Lake, Sacramento in spring 2018
● David Blink performed in the Mt Shasta 4th of July parade. This is laying the
groundwork for future performances in the festivities with COS ensembles.
● David Blink was Master of Ceremonies for the COS Scholarship Dinner with Dr. Ron
Slabbinck as Auctioneer (Fall 2018)
● David Blink gave a music workshop/performance for a special needs classroom at
Evergreen Elementary School in Yreka
● Enrollment in choral/vocal ensemble courses continue to increase and stabilize
○ Chamber Choir now consistently between 20 and 24 members
○ Concert Choir approaching 50 members
○ Number and skill level of students auditioning for vocal jazz ensemble continues
to increase
● Chamber Choir now engaging in outreach with local school and community
organizations (Gazelle, Weed, Butteville, Sisson, Boys and Girls Club, Weed High
School, Siskiyou Art Museum)
● Gave three performing arts presentations for the visiting Eagle Point Tour and various
middle schools
● Presented a community music workshop for the 8th Grade Introduction to COS. Also led
8th grade tours of the COS performing and visual arts area

-- 1 of 10 --

title: Music Annual Update _ CQIP 2018-2019 SY (Part 2)
text:
● David Blink was Master of Ceremonies for the Music by the Mountain Annual
Fundraising Event with Dr. Ron Slabbinck as Auctioneer (Summer 2018)
● Summer Music Showchoir/Jazz Camp now firmly re-established
● The following performances in and around the community aid in both recruiting and
retention. Performances expose potential future students to the music department. They
also lead to student retention, as performances help build community and purpose in the
students.
○ National Anthem at 3 Football Games – Music Students
○ Open Rehearsal - Senior Day @ COS
○ Open Rehearsal - Foster Care Youth Visit
○ Soroptomist Club – Vocal Jazz
○ Boys and Girls Club – Chamber Singers
○ Weed High School Choir – Chamber Singers
○ Dunsmuir Holiday Parade – Chamber Singers
○ Yreka Nursing Home – Vocal Jazz
○ COS Jazz Concert – Vocal Jazz/Jazz Band
○ Yreka Jazz Concert – Vocal Jazz/Jazz Band
○ COS Winter Concert – Concert Band, Concert Choir, Chamber Singers
○ Yreka Winter Concert – Concert Band, Concert Choir, Chamber Singers
○ COS Student Recital – Beginning Voice and Applied Music students
○ COS Orchestra Concert – Orchestra
○ Collaboration with Theatre Department on “Spamalot” (Seven Performances
during Fall 2017)
○ Collaboration with Theatre Department on “Little Shop of Horrors” (Eight
Performances during Fall 2018)
Retention - The items listed below create an environment in which students have a
continued positive, learner centered, rigorous experience. This experience creates a
program of which students want to be a part.
● Adjusted Applied Music from 4.9 LHE to 2.0 LHE, allowing for more contact time
between instructor and students
● Music Appreciation course has modified the curriculum to include project based learning
(PBL) and democratic classroom teaching techniques.

-- 2 of 10 --

title: Music Annual Update _ CQIP 2018-2019 SY (Part 3)
text:
● Applied Music has added democratic classroom teaching techniques
● Established the COS UKE Club in collaboration with Art Instructor Sean Kinney. This
club is also serving the community members through it’s weekly meetings.
● Applied Music Students and Faculty Performed music for the COS Drawing Class &
Clay Class (creating community on campus)
● Faculty and Applied Music students performed music for the Pottery Sale in the Lobby of
the Library (Spring 2018 & Fall 2018)
● Co-organized with Performing Arts faculty and students for the “Come Alive” flash-mob
during the 2018 COS commencement ceremony during the President’s speech (as
requested by the President)
● Continued to work with the President’s office on establishing TEDx at the College of the
Siskiyous
○ David Blink attended the TEDFest training in NYC in Spring 2018 to make
him/college eligible for a large site licensee.
● Concert Band/Jazz Band now year-round ensembles
○ Concert band has increased Woodwinds from 2 last year to 9 students
○ Jazz Band now has a complete instrumentation (which is a change from the past
10 years).
● Ron Slabbinck completed his doctorate in Educational Leadership. In the program, a
program focused on service learning and social justice, Ron completed a dissertation
designed to find ways to make music instruction in higher education more relevant and
meaningful for its students.
● David Blink led a delegation from the college to Morelia, Michoacan, Mexico along with
2 other faculty members and the Dean. This trip was to lay the groundwork for potential
future intersession international course offerings. Also made contacts with UNAM
Escuela Nacional de Estudios Superiores Unidad Morelia.
○ This trip was the first step in establishing a student exchange program with COS
and La Escuela Nacional de Estudios Superiores Unidad Morelia (UNAM)
○ Working on collaborations with the University of Hull (England), Washington
State University, UNAM, and COS for Science and Art research projects
associated with the Butterfly Colony in Mexico

-- 3 of 10 --

title: Music Annual Update _ CQIP 2018-2019 SY (Part 4)
text:
Completion (Awards, Degrees and Certification) - The following items lead toward higher
student achievement, success, and completion.
● Modified Music Theory Courses to be dual modality (Hybrid Face to face/Online)
● Music Degree Requirements/Course Sequence is in the process of being modified in
order to have a degree program that can be completed in two years and is rigorous as both
a stand alone degree and as preparation for transfer to a four year music degree program.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
Program Learning Outcomes
Additionally, the COS Music Department will strive to complete the following objectives:
● Model Music PLOs after Interstate Passport Learning Outcomes
● Modify Music Degree Course Requirements and Sequence
● Updating Courses (over the next year)
● Performing Arts will perform a flex activity for course mapping of the winter intersession
● Just as with the theatre department:
○ All courses will have a canvas Shell and all SLOs will be mapped to Assessments
○ Course Mapping, Assignments mapped to SLOs, SLOs mapped to PLOs
○ All courses should have rubrics for each assignment
○ Assessments in music courses need to be mapped to outcomes
Current COS CATALOGUE Program Outcomes
Upon successful completion of this program, the student should be able to:
● COS MUSIC DEPARTMENT
● Demonstrate an awareness of the ways in which people through the ages and in different
cultures have responded to themselves and the world around them in artistic and cultural
creation.
● Demonstrate the ability to analyze and appreciate works of philosophical, historical,
literary, aesthetic, and cultural importance.
● Demonstrate the ability to play piano at a grade-three level.
● Analyze works in traditional and non-traditional contexts.

-- 4 of 10 --

title: Music Annual Update _ CQIP 2018-2019 SY (Part 5)
text:
● Compose or arrange works in traditional and non-traditional contexts.
● Demonstrate the ability to sing at sight and transcribe at hearing traditional and non-
traditional music.
● In area of chosen focus, demonstrate proficiency at a comparable level to a student
entering upper division coursework.
● Demonstrate an understanding of the responsibilities of musicianship.
INTERSTATE PASSPORT OUTCOMES
● Identify, explain and/or demonstrate relationships among societal, cultural, and
historical contexts.
● Demonstrate knowledge of and empathy for the diversity of values, beliefs, ideas, and
practices embodied in the human experience.
● Use appropriate methods and tools to analyze, interpret and critique creative processes,
works, and/or presentations.
● Engage in a creative process through experimentation, reflection, tolerance for failure,
and revision.
● Employ fundamental discipline-specific principles, terminology, skills, technology, and
methods.
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
Obstacles
● Accompanist pay rate makes it difficult to recruit and retain collegiate level accompanists
○ One of our accompanists has stepped down from her role, citing the lack of a pay
rate increase for six years as the primary reason
○ Local Elementary and High Schools pay their accompanists $4-$8 per hour more
than the college
○ Consider reclassifying accompanists to EIA
■ Accompanists function similar to EIA’s when rehearsing with students
outside of class time
● Preparation for lessons
● Preparation for in class performances
● Preparation for recitals

-- 5 of 10 --

● Finding local qualified EIA for Applied Music Students
○ Pay rate has not changed for six years
○ Consider reclassifying to a higher scale rate
● Budgeting process clarification and transparency
○ All budget items zeroed out for FY19 except
■ 4130 - Instructional Supplies
■ 2411 - Instructional Aide
Support
● Transparency between the Dean of Instruction and music faculty has allowed for honest
and open dialogue regarding issues, mission, vision, short, and long term goals. The dean
has taken an appropriate role when needed, whether that role was one of support or one
of constructive criticism. And the dean has been professional and appropriate in all
situations.
● Clarification on budgeting process has enabled music faculty to develop a more
intentional, thoughtful, and student centered budget process.
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications. (Submit a CQIP if you are requesting a
budget allocation that falls outside of regular yearly budget allocations).
● 2112-1110 Faculty - Full Time
○ A full-time piano instructor/staff accompanist position is essential to the growth
and development of the music and theatre departments
● 2112-2126 Class-Overtime
○ For all music performances (on and off campus), Tech and stage support requires
additional staff
○ Twelve Concerts/year
○ Six Dress Rehearsals/year
○ Growth - Add 2-4 additional Concerts
● 2112-2211 Instr Aide (Instr, Reg) N/A
● 2112-2321 Student Help (Noninstr) - District
○ Create inventory of music and instruments
○ Assist with clerical/photocopying

-- 6 of 10 --

text:

○ No budget was provided for instrument repair, music, new equipment. This
impacts the ability of the instrumental program to maintain the current inventory
of instrument that the college owns. This directly impacts potential students in our
performing ensembles by not having working/maintained instruments
● Possible water damage to projector and equipment in T3 and hallway due to rain
● Absence of Instructor Stations in T1, T2, T3
○ Only one of the two full time instructors has a school issued laptop that can plug
into the projection system in T2 & T3
